- 123_Private VIF.txt:27: Now, you know that when you create a subnet, there is always a local route, right?
- 123_Private VIF.txt:32: Now, when you create a private VIF, and then you have a virtual private gateway, you can modify your subnet, and say that all the traffic, which goes to the 192.168.0.0/24, has to be going through the virtual private gateway, right?
- 123_Private VIF.txt:35: Now, for example, I have shown that, if it learned about three such prefixes, then, automatically, it says the traffic, for this destination, should be going through the virtual private gateway, and ultimately, through private VIF, to the corporate data center.
- 123_Private VIF.txt:36: And that's where, suppose now, you're EC2 is trying to reach to this machine, which has an IP address of 192.168.0/10.
- 123_Private VIF.txt:40: So, this is what I mean, when I say the propagated route will take precedence, over the internet gateway routes.
- 123_Private VIF.txt:46: So, when you have this connection, you have Direct Connect location, you have Direct Connect router, here, and from there you create a private VIF, and you attach to the virtual private gateway.
- 123_Private VIF.txt:51: Now, when this is the case, let's understand what you can access, from your on-premises network and what you cannot.
- 123_Private VIF.txt:78: And then there is a new option, to connect your private VIF to the Direct Connect gateway.
- 124_Transit VIF.txt:16: For example, in that case, you can set up the architecture which does this and just one more thing to remember is that because there are now 2 gateways, Transit gateway and DX gateway, you have to make sure that the AS Numbers for those are different.
- 124_Transit VIF.txt:20: And with respect to the ASN it should be different when you associate Transit Gateway and the DX Gateway.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:17: But here in this case, if you want to connect to different AWS Region VPCs, then you cannot with just the private VIF.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:29: But when you create a VIF, that VIF and the DX Gateway should be in the same AWS account.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:40: So when you associate this connection from AWS side, you will publish all the routes from your VPCs.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:52: But just for your understanding, when you design the architecture, you need to consider those limits moving on.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:60: And that's where now when your direct connect Gateway is created.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:61: So you can see that my private VIF would allow me to connect to that particular DirectConnect Gateway.
- 125_Direct Connect Gateway with Private VIF and VGW.txt:69: But if more than 20 VPCs you want to connect, then you can go with this kind of the architecture.
- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:3: And let's look at the architectures that you can build when you use these three components together.
- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:24: Whenever you are designing your architecture with respect to the routes, you can advertise up to 200 routes per Transit Gateway towards the on premise network.
- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:31: But when you create a Transit VIF or a DX Gateway, this should be a part of the same AWS account.
- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:40: Now this was also true when you were not using the Transit Gateway and you were just using the Direct Connect Gateway, attaching it to the VPC virtual private gateways and similar connectivity you get with the Transit gateways.
- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:57: And we had seen similar pattern when we were connecting DX Gateway directly to the VPCs.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:7: Now, essentially what it means that if you have, say, for example, your own customer data centers and there is no AWS.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:10: For example, if it's a small company, then they might just go over the Internet for this connectivity and you know that Internet cannot provide you the consistent bandwidth.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:13: And then you are locked in for many years and many a times these are like point to point connections and you don't get much of the flexibility when you are to expand your network to other geographies.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:19: For example, if you are in the US, then you might want to select a Boston one.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:69: It supports both IPv4 and IPv6 routing prefixes and traffic, and it can support different kind of architectures like a full mesh architecture where you have multiple sites and you want to connect to those, or you want to make sure that your one site is connected to a particular site only and a third site cannot access that data.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:71: So imagine you have your data centers across the world, you are really a multinational company and you want to connect all these data centers together with AWS Backbone Network.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:76: The other type of connectivity you might want to have is maybe you have your own data centers and you want to connect to your partner networks and you could have multiple partners and you don't want that.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:86: For example, there is a production network and there is a test setup network and you don't want production, network and test network to go over the same connection.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:94: For example, you will have another set of connection whips connecting to different direct connect gateway to redirect that traffic to the prod workload only so you can very well set this kind of isolated connectivity as well.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:106: Just one thing you need to remember if you want to connect multiple customer data centers using AWS Backbone Network or a direct connect, then you can use AWS Sitelink.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:3: When the traffic goes out from AWS to on-premises network, you know that traffic starts from the VPC and then VPC itself has a route table.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:7: Typically, whenever you have multiple DX connections to reach out to different destinations.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:17: Now, when VPC has to make a decision where the traffic will go, then there are certain route priorities that AWS has defined.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:21: So for example, if there is a route for a particular IP address and it goes through, for example, internet Gateway, then even if there is another route for 10.10.2.0/24 and you have defined target for that as a VPC Peering connection, then the route through the internet Gateway will take precedence because you have defined it as the longest prefix which it matches with, then comes the static routes as compared to the propagated routes.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:39: Now, if you look at the VPC route table, it would have the local route for the VPC CIDR itself and then multiple routes to reach to this CIDR block as shown in this route table.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:46: So this path is chosen to reach out to that machine.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:60: Now, now DX Routing policies and BGP communities are used to influence the Routing decision when the traffic flows through the direct connect connections.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:79: So when you advertise your routes from on-premises to AWS side, you can restrict that your routes should be advertised to only base AWS Region or to that continent or they should be propagated globally in all AWS public network.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:85: And additionally, you can also use local BGP community tags to influence the traffic when there are multiple paths to reach to the target network on your side.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:4: So in this case, of course, when you create a public wifi, you remember you have to provide the IPv4 prefixes or IPv6 prefixes, right?
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:25: And once that is done, you advertise these routes and these routes get advertised to all AWS regions because public wifi allows you to connect to all AWS regions.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:33: Now in this case, how AWS takes that decision, like I want to take one path over the other, and this is essentially applicable when there are multiple direct connect connections between AWS and your on-premise networks, right?
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:37: And if there are two connection to reach to the destination, which route it will take?
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:43: Essentially, this means how many hubs are there to reach to the destination.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:60: We just talked about that when we say there is a No Export BGP community tag, which AWS sends along with the routes.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:62: That means if you're multiple direct connect connection to reach to the target, you can decide if I want to do a load sharing of the traffic between these two connection, or maybe sometimes you want to have one connection as an active and one connection as a passive.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:64: So now with basic understanding of how the routing and BGP communities work, let's try to build some architecture when it comes to the traffic flowing between the AWS and on-premise network and when there are multiple direct connect connections.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:9: So considering the case, when you're using a public ASN for public VIF, then how the routing happens is that, on the customer gateway site, you have to advertise the same prefix with the same border gateway protocol.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:16: That means you can only use the private ASN for use cases like active-passive connection.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:23: Now, if this is the architecture, then there are two parts to reach to AWS, as well as from AWS to optimize.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:28: Now when we say same BGP prefix as in, when you advertise your prefix information, then it should be same.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:46: AS_Path has the hops, like how many hops it takes to reach to the destination.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:50: Now, when AWS learns about this BGP parameters, it will take always the primary connection as, you know, the first choice, if the primary connection is done, then it will look for other paths.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:73: Of course there is another part that AWS knows how to reach to your router, so it will take the other part.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:74: Now, this is a case when AWS wants to send the traffic to your router, right?
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:87: This is all when you are using the public ASN for your router.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:90: So for example, you will send something like a base address/25 here.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:102: Now for active-passive connection, things change a little when you use a public ASN or a private ASN.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:108: But then this is always good to know when you actually work on direct connect, and when you actually get a chance to set up those routes, right?
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:8: So typically when you receive the on-premises prefixes or routes or a BGP session on AWS site, you have a choice or you have a control where those routes can be propagated to all AWS regions or you know particular AWS region, for example, and the requirement could be, you only want to have the connection with one AWS region because your company do not operate or do not use other AWS regions so there is no point in opening the routes for the other regions to communicate with your on-premise network.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:16: So if I just want to show here, whenever routes are advertised from customer router to AWS customer route can set any of this BGP communities and when AWS sends the route of AWS public IPS and prefixes to the customer network, it can also set this BGP community tags.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:24: So for example, there is North America as a continent.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:56: Now, this is something like, when you receive the routes at your end, you can understand that these routes am receiving from a local AWS region in which I have Direct Connect connection.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:92: And there could be also routes sent by the public region like North Virginia, which is not part of the same content, in that case from Ireland, when the routes are received, it has the community tag as 72248200.

- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:101: So you want to only have the communication with local AWS region in that case, what you'll see, you'll see the routes coming in, say for example, routes from the Ireland region, and you will see that there is a BGP community tag 8200 but I don't want to have my communication with the Ireland region.

- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:5: And when we say private, that means there is actually a dedicated line as well from direct connect location to your data center.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:12: Now, that would depend on at which layer you want to encrypt that traffic.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:14: So if you want to use or say, if you want to encrypt the traffic at layer three of the OSI layer, then you will use VPN over a direct connect.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:19: Now, apart from this, if you want to encrypt the traffic at above layer, like layer four, you can always use say STPs kind of protocol, which ultimately uses TLS at layer four to encrypt the traffic.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:25: So the ideal way to encrypt the traffic is to have layer four that is transport layer security encryption enabled on the host communicating over the direct connect.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:56: Now, just one more thing about this architecture is that you might be wondering that we don't have the direct gateway here in between, because you know that if you want to connect transit gateway to the direct connect connection, then you need to have the direct connect gateway.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:79: And just one more point about the MACSec is that when you configure your connection, you can specify one of the values for your MACSec and those values are, should encrypt, must encrypt, or no encrypt.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:80: So what this should encrypt means is that it will try to encrypt the traffic.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:93: So with that, for the security of the direct connect connection, you have multiple options to encrypt the traffic.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:96: But in case you need to encrypt this traffic at layer three or layer two, then you have two options.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:4: And then we also saw when it comes to the VPC, what is an MTU that is supported when the traffic flows over to the internet or between VPCs over a VPC peering connection or through a VPN connection, right.

- 138_DX Pricing.txt:2: Now, this is one of the most confusing topic, I would say, when it comes to the Direct Connect Billing.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:8: Okay, so when it comes to the Direct Connect, you know what parties are involved, there is AWS region, which is connected to on-premise data center via the Direct Connect location, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:12: Now in all this, what are the charges that you have to pay when you go for a Direct Connect.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:21: Now, when we say data charges out means, any data that is going from AWS to on-premise network, or to Direct Connect location, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:50: So for example, if source region is not Virginia, and the destination DX location is Las Vegas, then the data transfer OUT charges are $0.02 per GB.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:67: Now, you need to understand that when it's a dedicated connection, you request for a connection and then you get LOA CFA, that you submit it to your Direct Connect partner, or a Direct Connect location provider.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:82: Also, you need to understand that, the billing for port-hour stops when the dedicated connection or hosted connection is deleted from your AWS account, which means, if it is just in a downstate, does not mean that there is no charge, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:104: So, this is a pretty simple when it is a private VIF, or a public VIF.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:117: So, this is one difference, when it comes to the transit gateway.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:119: If there are multiple AWS organization involved, for example, the Direct Connect connection is owned by AWS account, which is part of AWS organization one, and the resource which is sending the data, is a part of an AWS account, which is part of AWS organization two.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:152: So with that, I think, we saw lot of things when it comes to the DX billing.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:153: And I think it is one of the, again, important topic to know, because then you're very clear, you know, when it comes to the questions related to the billing for DX So, thanks for watching.

- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:8: And in all likelihood, you would want to connect all these data centers to AWS regions and applications running inside those regions as well.

- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:18: Now examples could be if you are running high performance cloud that is HPC compute or you are working on some media kind of application like Netflix for example, you are uploading or downloading a lot of data or you are running some AV.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:35: You also want to connect your data centers to the transit gateways, and you already know that you can do that using the Transit Gateway and Direct Connect Gateway.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:56: We could have, but we are not, because Transit Gateway has a lot of charge when it comes to per GB data processing.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:65: And finally, if you want to connect to the public services, like, for example, S3, again, you don't want to send that data through Transit Gateway or through your VPC because there would be additional cost.

### 5. Constraints / Limitations
- 092_Introduction to Hybrid networking.txt:2: Now, hybrid networking is one of the most important topic for your exam.
- 092_Introduction to Hybrid networking.txt:4: But the most important AWS services are AWS Site-to-Site VPN and AWS Direct Connect.
- 092_Introduction to Hybrid networking.txt:6: And if required, you can also try to implement the exercises that we are going to cover in this section.
- 092_Introduction to Hybrid networking.txt:30: Okay, so before we get into this topics, I think it's very important that you understand the basics of the routing and what I mean.
- 092_Introduction to Hybrid networking.txt:32: And then you should also understand how the border gateway protocol works, and then how the border gateway attributes impact the routing decision which BGP makes.
- 093_Static Routing vs Dynamic Routing.txt:28: Now these are two different networks and in order for the packet to route from network A to network B, of course, there should be a route table for router A which has an entry for target destination as a network B, and the target should be router B.
- 093_Static Routing vs Dynamic Routing.txt:30: Now similarly at network B, you should also have the route table, but this time the local route would be itself.
- 094_How Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) works_.txt:8: Now, border gateway protocol is also important with respect to your exam.
- 094_How Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) works_.txt:32: So you can define the weight on the different paths, and then the routing would be taken to the highest weight, but that only works in the Cisco routers.
- 094_How Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) works_.txt:80: They learn the route from the other AS, like if from AS 100, I want to reach to AS 400, then what route entry I should have.
- 094_How Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) works_.txt:97: That means right now, I can't reach to that destination.
- 094_How Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) works_.txt:117: Now, BGP can't take other path because in routing table, it knows that there is one more path exists to reach to the target destination.
- 095_BGP Route selection - ASPATH, LOCAL_PREF, MED.txt:48: The question is which route it should take.
- 095_BGP Route selection - ASPATH, LOCAL_PREF, MED.txt:51: And as I'm showing here, this decision is only within that AS.
- 095_BGP Route selection - ASPATH, LOCAL_PREF, MED.txt:63: Now, Multi Exit Discriminator will come into the picture, when local preference is the same, when ASPATH is the same, then only this is MED is looked at.
- 095_BGP Route selection - ASPATH, LOCAL_PREF, MED.txt:64: Now, if you can see, of course there is only one hop between both these, that means ASPATH is same.
- 095_BGP Route selection - ASPATH, LOCAL_PREF, MED.txt:77: So for influencing the BGP routing decision, there is something called Highest Weight, but this is only specific to the Cisco routers.
- 096_Introduction to AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:42: That means using the private IPS without those resources required to have the public IPS.
- 096_Introduction to AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:90: However, if you talk about the IPsec VPN, it supports only unicast.
- 096_Introduction to AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:91: That means it connects just two networks and it can only transport the IP packets.
- 096_Introduction to AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:99: So you can only attach single virtual private gateway to a VPC.
- 096_Introduction to AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:122: But before that, I think one important thing to know is that how does AWS VPN support the IPv4 and IPv6 traffic?
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:14: And this will be public IP addresses because otherwise from customer gateway you cannot connect to AWS, right?
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:15: So there should be some public connectivity for this tunnel endpoints.
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:32: These outside IPS are only used for the connection.
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:63: And this configuration is only supported on the Transit Gateway and AWS cloud Wan.
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:67: Then you should use this configuration.
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:68: Whereas if you are not using IPv6 addresses at all, then you should use this first configuration, right?
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:71: That means your applications can be reached over IPv6 addresses, but tunnel can only support IPv4 traffic.
- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:72: And again, this is only supported on the Transit Gateway and AWS cloud van.
- 098_Hands On_ Setup AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:3: And then you should be able to access these servers on both the sides over their private IPs.

- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:1: Okay, now that you know how AWS Site to Site VPN works, let's look at some of the features and one of the feature of site to site VPN that is important to know is accelerated site to site VPN.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:17: And then your VPN connection might get terminated or it might not give the required bandwidth.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:40: And then we will talk about some important things that you should know about.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:45: Now remember that acceleration is only supported on the transit gateways, so it is not supported on Virtual Private gateway.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:51: Now another thing to remember is that you can only select this option while creating the VPN connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:52: Once you create that you can't change that later, right?
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:55: So going back to our discussion, a few important things that you need to know is that it is only supported on the transit gateway.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:56: That means if you are terminating your VPN on the virtual private gateways, then you can't have this VPN acceleration.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:57: Second, it can only be enabled while creating the VPN connection, which means that you can't enable that on the existing VPN connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:58: So in your exam, if there is a question which says that you want to accelerate the VPN, in that case, you can't really do that on existing connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:59: So the answer should be you should create a new connection and that too should terminate on AWS Transit Gateway.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:69: Now another important thing for having the VPN acceleration you have to have Nat traversal enabled.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:77: So this is another thing that you should know.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:80: So I hope it is clear what is accelerated site to site VPN, and what is the purpose and what are some important thing that you should remember.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:82: And with that, let's talk about some other important features of AWS site to site VPN connection.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:2: The another important feature that you should know about AWS site to Site VPN is the Nat traversal, also written as Nat T.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:6: And important thing to note is that the customer gateway where we are terminating the VPN is publicly accessible.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:17: So in the regular case, if your customer gateway is publicly accessible, then you must allow inbound and outbound traffic for this two protocols and the ports UDP with 500 ports and ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:22: Now if this is the case basically Nat device cannot handle the ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:25: And that's where you cannot directly use the ESP 50 protocol here.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:40: So for Nat traversal, no configuration is required on AWS side, but on the customer gateway side.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:41: If you are not using Nat, then you must allow inbound and outbound for UDP 500 and ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:42: But if your customer gateway is behind the net, then you must allow inbound and outbound traffic on UDP port 500 and UDP port 4500.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:4: Now, in case of Static VPN, what happens, like you have to update the route tables on both sides of the connection, and you have to say like, if the traffic is going to particular destination on the right side, then the batch, the traffic should go through Virtual Private Gateway and similarly, route table you need to maintain on the Customer Gateway side, that if the destination is AWS CIDR, then the traffic should go through the Customer Gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:8: In that case on AWS side, you need to update your route table for your subnets of course and tell that if the traffic is going for that network C, then also it should take the path through VPN gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:13: Now, one thing to note, there is that AWS side of the route tables, that means the route table, which are attached to subnets, cannot have more than a hundred propagated routes.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:14: Now that's a limitation on AWS side, which means if on the customer side, if you are sending more than hundred routes through BGP, your connection might break, and we don't know how the exact behavior it would be, but the chances are your VPN connection can get terminated because AWS side of the network cannot tolerate or cannot accept more than hundred propagated routes.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:23: It says if the traffic is going to 10.10.0016 which is a VPC CIDR, the traffic should go through the Customer Gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:26: So remember that on AWS side, you can only accept a hundred routes from the customer network side.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:16: But in this case, VGW is not ENI, it's a gateway and that's where it can't route the traffic directly from VGW to IGW.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:18: That means you cannot reach to the internet via the VGW, remember this.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:19: Now, moving on, what if now, instead of we said that you cannot go directly to the IGW, but what if we introduced NAT gateway in between?
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:22: And due to NAT gateway limitations, we cannot still reach to the internet if the traffic lands to VGW, and then from there, we can't really go to the internet via NAT gateway.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:27: So remember, for sending the traffic to internet via IGW, you can't use internet gateway directly or NAT gateway directly, but you can have your NAT EC2 instance and then that traffic can flow.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:32: That means if traffic is landing into this VPC from outside, this traffic cannot flow to the other peered VPC directly, unless you are originating that traffic here in the primary VPC.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:42: This traffic cannot flow through virtual private gateway.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:44: We said this is an invalid scenario, where traffic is coming from on-premise through virtual private gateway, or say VPN connection, you cannot reach to AWS services through a gateway endpoint.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:48: Now, if you see, I have some destination in my VPC where the traffic can land, and from there through that ENI, I should be able to go to the respective services.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:65: So if traffic is originating from your on-premises and going to AWS via virtual private gateway, then you cannot access internet through a VPC attached internet gateway, you cannot access internet through a NAT gateway, you cannot access peered VPC resources through peering connection, and you cannot access S3 and DynamoDB via the VPC gateway endpoint.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:3: Now, you know that AWS site-to-site VPN provisions two tunnels, that's for high availability and it's up to you how you configure this tunnels, weather in active-active mode, which means both the tunnels are up at the same time and you can also configure it in active-passive mode, that means only one tunnel is up at any moment of the time.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:18: Now, if your traffic originates on AWS side of the network, for example, this EC2 instance here wants to send some traffic to any of those servers in corporate data center, it will send the packet to virtual private gateway and then this VGW will randomly select one of the tunnel, either tunnel 1 or tunnel two, you can't really influence that decision in case of static routing.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:22: Now what that means is, only one of the tunnel up at any moment of the time.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:23: So tunnel two is for example down, if you remember our hands-on exercise, we had set up opens one tunnel and we had configured only one tunnel at that time, we did not configure another tunnel.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:25: Now in this case, let's walk through the same scenario again, from your corporate data center if traffic goes to AWS first, it will go to customer gateway from there only one tunnel is up, there is no choice and you have to get to the VGW, VGW will send traffic to EC2 and from there return traffic will come to VGW, now VGW will see that there is only tunnel up, so there is no choice to go through another tunnel of course it will send traffic back from the same tunnel through which the traffic came.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:26: So in that case, you don't really need to worry about asymmetric routing because there is only one active tunnel.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:32: AWS does not have to do anything there, this is completely in your control, which tunnel should be up at any moment.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:37: So, this time, we have active-active tunnels and it would make sense to have only active-active tunnels because then there is no purpose of having two tunnels for high availability, right?
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:51: Now this is only possible in case of dynamic routing because you are having to BGP peers, one at VGW and other at CGW end.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:56: So this is very interesting and very important to know for you, may not be directly asked in a exam, but maybe in your day to day work you can analyze why you have asymmetric routing and how to fix that.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:59: In case of static routing with active-passive tunnel mode, that means only one tunnel is up at the moment, you don't have to really worry because always that tunnel will be used for both sides of the communication.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:61: Now in case of dynamic routing, of course it will make sense to have active-active tunnel mode only because, active-passive, you are just not leveraging the high availability of the tunnels.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:3: And this is important because many times probably you want to fail-over to another channel or another network for your VPN connection or for sometimes, maybe you want to close that connection due to security reasons.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:16: As I said, VGW will send R-U-THERE messages continuously and your customer gateway should response to those messages.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:29: Customer, which uses the Dynamic Routing must support the DPD at their router.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:46: So that's it about DPD important for your exam to have a fail-over scenarios.

- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:3: Now, this is the most important section of this entire course because most of the questions are around the hybrid connectivity.
- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:4: And direct connect is one of the important aspect of the hybrid connectivity.
- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:31: On top of that physical connection, there should be an Ethernet and it should be supporting the VLAN tagging.
- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:41: And that's where I said the layers are more theoretical and you can't really map 1 to 1 mapping between the layers and the protocols.
- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:48: But you can't directly put that private network from your on premise data center to AWS, right?
- 113_Introduction to AWS Direct Connect.txt:49: You can't lay down those wires there directly for that.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:6: This is required for the physical connectivity between your data center and AWS direct connect location.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:10: Second, at Layer2, you need to have 802.1Q VLAN encapsulation protocol enabled and we'll talk about that third, auto negotiation of the ports must be disabled.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:11: DX supports the BGP protocol and your side of the router should also support the BGP protocol with MD5 authentication.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:22: And for DirectConnect, you must support this protocol.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:44: So for DX connections which are greater than 1 Gbps port speed, this setting should be disabled.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:45: But what this setting means is that, when two networking devices wants to communicate with each other, they will negotiate few settings like what should be the port speed, what should be the duplex mode, whether it should be half duplex or full duplex.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:49: And for the connections which are 1 Gbps or less, it depends on the router vendor, whether this setting should be enabled or disabled.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:52: So DX supports BGP and at your side of the router should also support the BGP protocol.

- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:64: So those are the technical requirements for DX connection just to reiterate again, you should have single mode fiber.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:66: You should have Layer2 protocol that is 802.1 Q VLAN encapsulation.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:67: The auto negotiation for the ports must be disabled for the connection greater than 1 Gbps.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:68: And then most important is that your router should support the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) with MD5 authentication.

- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:5: So this discussion is for public VIF only.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:8: So typically when you receive the on-premises prefixes or routes or a BGP session on AWS site, you have a choice or you have a control where those routes can be propagated to all AWS regions or you know particular AWS region, for example, and the requirement could be, you only want to have the connection with one AWS region because your company do not operate or do not use other AWS regions so there is no point in opening the routes for the other regions to communicate with your on-premise network.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:9: Now in that case you can control that advertisement that I am sending these routes from my customer gateway, but these route should be advertised to only the primary AWS region where the direct connection is established and do not advertise it to other AWS regions.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:21: So if you set the community tag 72249100, then those prefixes or routes are advertised only local AWS region where their direct connection is located, right?
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:30: If you think that you operate or use only North America based US regions, then you will set the BGP community tag is 9200.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:35: Now, if you are using the community tag as 92249100, then all the routes received are only published or advertised to the local AWS region in which the Direct Connect connection is located.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:37: So only this particular region would receive that route.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:38: And which means that you can't really access the public AWS services from other AWS regions, because they don't know how to route traffic back to on-premises.

- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:97: Now, what you can do is you will receive this routes as well as BGP communities at your end and with the NO_EXPORT, you can't really have this advertised to other route table or a public route table which are all over the internet, right?

- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:5: So let's see what's the purpose and why should you use the lags.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:13: So for creating the lags your connection should be dedicated connections.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:15: Which means if you have hosted DCS connection then you cannot create the lag on that.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:16: Now it can aggregate up to maximum four connections only and those connections will be in active mode.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:18: Now all connections in the lags must have the same bandwidth, so you cannot mix like one GB s connection and ten GB s connection.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:19: All should be one GB, all should be ten GB or 100 GB s, and all these connections should be terminating on the same DCS location.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:22: So this is an important consideration while creating the lag.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:28: Now let's look at quickly few architectures and few more things about the lags that you should know for your exam.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:36: But as you go to the 100 GB s then you cannot have 400 GB s as your aggregated bandwidth.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:37: As of today, AWS only allows to have 200 GB s connections to be a part of the lag.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:39: So while using the 100 GB s, there is this difference that you should understand.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:51: If one of the connection fails then should my lag also show the status as failed or my lag should keep operating.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:54: But you can define the operational parameters, which defines the minimum number of connections that should be active for lag to be active.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:67: Okay, just one more thing that you should know for the lag is that you can enable the macsec with the lag.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:73: Now only the difference is that if you use existing connection to create a lag, and on that connection, if you have the macsec already enabled, then that macsec key is dis associated with the connection first, and the lag key instead associated with that connection.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:74: So this is just one thing that you should be knowing.
- 134_DX Connection Resiliency.txt:7: In this case, if the network connection between AWS and on-premise data center is really critical, then you should also design the high availability of the Direct Connect connections.
- 134_DX Connection Resiliency.txt:19: Now, this is important because if both of the connections dominate on the same AWS Direct Connect device, then the failure in that device will cause, again, the disruption of both the connections.
- 134_DX Connection Resiliency.txt:40: So, important that you decide what kind of workloads you have, can they withstand the network failure, then, what is your RTO recovery time for that, and what's the cost implication of having the multiple Direct Connect connections?

- 135_DX Failure detection with BFD.txt:9: So while using the multiple connection or using VPN backup, it's always important to have fast failure to a redundant connection.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:3: Now, there isn't much there to talk about, but then again, it's important for your exam.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:7: Now with that, sometimes it's still required to have the traffic to be encrypted, what flows over through direct connect.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:26: So host should handle that encryption and not the PII, which is taking your packet from source to the destination.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:47: Now one thing to understand is that you are still limited by the bandwidth of VGW, you know that VGW provides maximum bandwidth of 1.25 GBPS per tunnel.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:48: And that's where even if you have the direct connect connection over, say, 100 Gbps, but you would be able to use only 1.25 Gbps.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:49: And that's what the limitation of this architecture, if you are using the VGW to terminate your VPN, that means you can use transit gateway as well, instead of VGW.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:51: Now transit gateway does not apply that limit of 1.25 Gbps.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:52: You can aggregate all your VGW limits into one transit gateway, and then you can get aggregate bandwidth out of multiple VGWs.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:55: And also you are not limited by the VGW bandwidth.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:67: And typically this is what is required to make sure the connection is very secure.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:68: So it provides the layer two security for dedicated connections only.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:70: It cannot be applied on the hosted connections.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:79: And just one more point about the MACSec is that when you configure your connection, you can specify one of the values for your MACSec and those values are, should encrypt, must encrypt, or no encrypt.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:80: So what this should encrypt means is that it will try to encrypt the traffic.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:87: But if you sent this MACSec parameter to must encrypt, so it will attempt to have the key handshake.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:91: And only the non-encrypted frames will be transmitted over the direct connect connection.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:94: Obviously, the first choice should be encrypting at layer four.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:103: And second, the MACSec security protocol is only applicable for the dedicated connection.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:3: And if you remember, we said that Jumbo Frames allows the larger packet size to flow over the network, which ultimately improves the network performance and the throughput because less number of packets are required to transmit the data.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:17: And this is required that when you have to use Jumbo Frames on the VIFs of course the underlying Direct Connect connection or LAG should also be supporting the Jumbo Frames.

- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:2: Now, this would be again helpful for your exam because, given a scenario, you should be able to know where exactly to look for solving that particular problem, or an issue.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:25: Now those IPs must be owned by the customer, you know that, so you are to make sure it is correct, VLAN IDs are correct.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:27: You know that if it's the same connection VLAN tags should be unique across all VIFs on the same connection.

- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:44: Then you know that in case of private VIF you can't advertise, or you shouldn't advertise more than 100 prefixes.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:45: Otherwise the BGP session goes down and for public VIF this limit is 1000 prefixes.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:46: So no more than 1000 prefixes should be advertised from customer into AWS side.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:53: Now for public VIF it should be publicly routable prefixes.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:56: And you should also check your VPC Route table that there is route exist to take that traffic from AWS to on-premise network.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:58: So make sure those routes are available, without that traffic cannot flow in either direction.

### 6. Patterns / Architectures

- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:3: Now, you know that AWS site-to-site VPN provisions two tunnels, that's for high availability and it's up to you how you configure this tunnels, weather in active-active mode, which means both the tunnels are up at the same time and you can also configure it in active-passive mode, that means only one tunnel is up at any moment of the time.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:6: Now this is a very much familiar architecture, where you have VPC and a corporate data center and they're connected using site-to-site VPN connection, on AWS side, you have virtual private gateway and on the customer network side, you have customer gateway and we have shown two tunnels here, that's what VGW does, right?
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:21: Now, again, we will look at the static routing but this time we have active-passive tunnels.

- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:38: If it's an active-passive, then it's kind of static routing you're doing, if tunnel goes down, you would have to manually bring up the tunnel.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:57: So, that's it about the active-active and active-passive routing.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:59: In case of static routing with active-passive tunnel mode, that means only one tunnel is up at the moment, you don't have to really worry because always that tunnel will be used for both sides of the communication.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:61: Now in case of dynamic routing, of course it will make sense to have active-active tunnel mode only because, active-passive, you are just not leveraging the high availability of the tunnels.

## Step 3 â€” Deep Expansion (Hybrid Connectivity Concepts)

### AWS Direct Connect
- What it is: Dedicated private network transport from enterprise/on-prem networks into AWS.
- Why it exists: Delivers consistent latency, bandwidth, and private path control.
- Internal working: Physical connection maps to virtual interfaces and BGP route exchange.
- Architecture: Redundant circuits, diverse locations, and backup VPN for failure scenarios.
- Key components: Private/Public/Transit VIF, DXGW, LAG, BFD, MACsec patterns.

### Site-to-Site VPN
- What it is: Managed IPsec tunnel pair connecting AWS with on-prem or other networks.
- Why it exists: Fast hybrid onboarding and encrypted backup path design.
- Internal working: Tunnel establishment, route exchange, and failover monitoring govern path selection.
- Architecture: Dual tunnels with health-aware routing and DX backup/overlay combinations.
- Key components: VGW/TGW attachment, customer gateway, NAT-T, DPD, route propagation.

### AWS Client VPN
- What it is: Managed remote-user VPN service for workforce access into AWS and connected networks.
- Why it exists: Centralizes user-to-network access with policy-based control and split/full tunnel options.
- Internal working: Client sessions authenticate and receive route/domain settings tied to endpoint policy.
- Architecture: Remote user access into dedicated landing VPC with segmented route targets.
- Key components: Endpoint, auth model, authorization rules, routes, split tunnel settings.

## Cross-Cloud Mapping (Concept-First)

| Underlying Concept | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Private dedicated enterprise link | Direct Connect | ExpressRoute | Cloud Interconnect |
| Encrypted site-to-site hybrid path | Site-to-Site VPN | VPN Gateway | HA VPN |
| Remote-user managed VPN | AWS Client VPN | P2S VPN / Entra-backed access patterns | BeyondCorp / Client VPN patterns |
| Dynamic route exchange | BGP across VPN/DX/TGW | BGP across ER/VPN hubs | BGP across Interconnect/VPN |

### Trade-offs and Decision Notes
- Dedicated links improve determinism but usually still need encrypted or VPN-based backup paths.
- BGP policy and advertisement control determine whether failover behaves correctly during real incidents.
- Hybrid success is as much routing governance as circuit provisioning.

## Phase-wise Architect-Level Organization

### Phase 1 â€” Foundations
- Establish routing ownership, advertisement intent, and encryption requirements first.
- Separate dedicated transport design from fallback and remote-user access design.

### Phase 2 â€” Core Services
- Implement Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, and Direct Connect with explicit route policy.
- Validate reachability, failover triggers, and auth boundaries for all hybrid paths.

### Phase 3 â€” Advanced Patterns
- Combine DX primary with VPN backup and segmented transit attachments.
- Use BGP and detection timers carefully to avoid unstable failover behavior.

### Phase 4 â€” System Design
- Reference architecture: redundant private transport + encrypted backup + controlled remote user access.
- Scaling considerations: route advertisement sprawl, tunnel lifecycle, overlapping prefixes, auth model drift.
- Cost considerations: circuit capacity, data transfer, VPN processing, operational support complexity.

## Hands-On Practice (Kid-Friendly Step-by-Step)

### Safety First
- Use a sandbox account only.
- Set a budget alert before creating resources.
- Delete resources after practice to avoid surprise cost.

- Signal 3: And similarly, let's launch another instance that is EC2B for testing the connectivity.

### Service-Specific Lab Paths
### Activity 1: Amazon VPC Lab for Hybrid routing foundations, BGP, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, resiliency and encryption patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> VPC
- Azure equivalent: Azure Virtual Network
- GCP equivalent: VPC Network
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> VPC.
3. Build: Create a VPC with one public and one private subnet.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Validate route table associations.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete test subnets and VPC components.

### Activity 2: Amazon EC2 Lab for Hybrid routing foundations, BGP, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, resiliency and encryption patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances
- Azure equivalent: Azure Virtual Machines
- GCP equivalent: Compute Engine
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances.
3. Build: Launch one small instance with default VPC.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Check instance running state and system status checks.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Terminate instance and remove extra volumes.

### Activity 3: Amazon CloudWatch Lab for Hybrid routing foundations, BGP, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, resiliency and encryption patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> CloudWatch
- Azure equivalent: Azure Monitor
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Monitoring
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> CloudWatch.
3. Build: Create one dashboard and one alarm.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Trigger alarm condition and inspect alarm history.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete dashboard and alarm.

### Activity 4: Elastic Load Balancing Lab for Hybrid routing foundations, BGP, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, resiliency and encryption patterns
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Load Balancers
- Azure equivalent: Azure Load Balancer or Application Gateway
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Load Balancing
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Load Balancers.
3. Build: Create an application load balancer with one target group.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Check target health and ALB DNS response.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete ALB, listeners, and target group.

### Quick Troubleshooting
- Access denied: verify role permissions or ask admin for least-privilege lab policy.
- Resource limit reached: pick another region or request quota increase.
- Unexpected charges: stop/delete resources and review cost explorer immediately.

## Cross-Cloud Thinking Mode

Use this lens to study Hybrid routing foundations, BGP, Site-to-Site VPN, Client VPN, Direct Connect, resiliency and encryption patterns in a cloud-agnostic way: focus on capability first, provider name second.

### Capability Mapping (AWS / Azure / GCP)
| Capability | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity | IAM / IAM Identity Center | Microsoft Entra ID + RBAC | Cloud IAM |
| Object Storage | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Virtual Machines | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Serverless Functions | AWS Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions / Cloud Run |
| Containers | Amazon EKS / ECS | AKS | GKE / Cloud Run |
| Databases | RDS / DynamoDB / ElastiCache | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB / Azure Cache | Cloud SQL / Firestore / Memorystore |
| Networking | VPC / Route 53 / ELB | VNet / DNS / Load Balancer | VPC / Cloud DNS / Cloud Load Balancing |
| Observability | CloudWatch / X-Ray / CloudTrail | Azure Monitor / App Insights / Activity Log | Cloud Monitoring / Cloud Logging / Cloud Trace |

### Same Lab Across 3 Clouds
| Lab Theme | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amazon VPC | Amazon VPC | Azure Virtual Network | VPC Network |
| Amazon EC2 | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Amazon CloudWatch | Amazon CloudWatch | Azure Monitor | Cloud Monitoring |
| Elastic Load Balancing | Elastic Load Balancing | Azure Load Balancer or Application Gateway | Cloud Load Balancing |

### How To Think Cross-Cloud
1. Start with workload requirement: latency, throughput, security, and compliance.
2. Map requirement to capability family (identity, network, compute, data, observability).
3. Compare service behavior, not branding: limits, scaling model, and failure modes.
4. Keep a portable architecture note: what is generic vs provider-specific.
5. Test same mini-lab in at least 2 clouds and write down differences.

### Child-Friendly Practice Challenge
- Pick one simple task (for example: upload one file, run one function, read one metric).
- Do the same task in AWS, Azure, and GCP.
- Fill this mini template:
  - What was the same?
  - What had a different name?
  - What felt easier/harder and why?
